comments.title: Thoughts on Spiritual Dryness and Questions on Astrological Primary Meanings
comments.content:
Good Evening,

For our Jewish friends, a happy Passover, if that's the appropriate wish for the holiday, and for everyone else, a happy Earth Day!

To Share: 1. A quick thought on the book: The Wealth of Nature is very likely my favorite of your non-spiritual (well, not as directly, anyway) books. The model of "Primary, Secondary, and Tertiary" economies has fundamentally changed how I look at economic questions, and your breakdown of the importance of concentration of energy is what finally broke my last shred of faith in the technological side of the myth of progress. So, thanks much for that.

Also, you might be tickled to know that I sneak an overview of the three economies into a lecture I give in a Business Communication class to a bunch of aspiring consultants and investment bankers (properly cited, of course). If I'm going to be a cog making cogs in the PMC machine, I can at least try to give them a glimpse outside the machine.

2. I've been struggling with a bit of the ol' spiritual dryness lately, and I thought I'd share a few thoughts on my experience, both to sort it out for myself, and in hopes it might prove helpful to others: https://jpowellrussell.com/#on_spiritual_dryness

To Ask: A few weeks back, in answer to a question on the three "primaries" in a natal chart (https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/273418.html?thread=47537930#cmt47537930), you mentioned that you treat the Sun sign as the personality, the rising sign as the Individuality, and the Moon as the subconscious/subjective self. Two questions on this, if I may:
1. Does this come from any specific set of teachings, or is it more a conclusion you've come to on your own? Either way, any recommendations on where to dig into this more, besides via meditation?
2. Does this imply that incarnation after incarnation, a soul would have the same rising sign, or is it more that the rising sign of any given life sums up the state of the Individuality going into that life, which will change and grow from one incarnation to the next? Or something else?

As always, thanks very much to JMG and everyone else here for all that you do.

My blessings to all who welcome them,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Thoughts on Spiritual Dryness and Questions on Astrological Primary Meanings
replies.content:
1) It's more or less the conclusion I've reached from studying various astrology texts. My only recommendation is to study your own natal chart and those of other people you know.

2) The individuality evolves and develops in each life. With this in mind, John Gilbert used to say that your rising sign in this life was your sun sign in your last life, since it's the most recent imprint on your individuality.

Hi Debi,
I also have moon in Aries. I'm not an astrologer but my sister is and I've picked up bits and pieces from her.

My understanding is that the moon influences emotional expression. Aries is a cardinal fire sign. Cardinal signs are the initiators, especially Aries, since it is the first sign of the zodiac. Cardinal signs are the leaders and the first to take action on an idea or project. They like to get things going but may prefer others to carry on with the project once it's started. Fire signs are passion, creativity, and intensity. Cardinal fire flares up quickly but also gets over it quickly; it doesn't do the slow burn (Leo: fixed fire) or dance all over the place (Sagittarius; mutable fire).

That kind of passion maybe at odds with the Pisces sun since Pisces is mutable water and has deep feelings but would probably prefer everything on the surface to be smooth and happy.

As I said I'm not an astrologer so I'm perfectly willing to be corrected on any of this.
Best wishes,
E.C.

comments.title: Self-immolation
comments.content:
Hi JMG, pursuant to ongoing sociopolitical turmoil there have been two recent incidents of people burning themselves to death outside of official facilities as extreme acts of protest. I recall there were people who did this during the Iraq war and they all seemed to be inspired by the deaths of Buddhist monks from Vietnam.

Can this act be seen as the infliction of a death curse on the entity targeted by the protestor? You've mentioned before that a person wishing to create destructive magical effects is most effective when they accept any and all ruinous blowback from the working, not trying to dodge any of it. I could see self-immolation as the most extreme form of this approach. It prefaces the working with one of the worst possible consequences, a horrific and painful death, in order to inflict maximum ruin upon the target. The image of these dying protesters is likely to linger in the thoughts of many people, which I would expect to boost the effect.

It's also interesting when looking at the intersection of politics and metaphysics. If these people physically attacked the entities they opposed, they could be branded as criminal and barbaric, but by attacking entirely on the metaphysical level at the cost of their own lives they block off this counter-narrative. At the least it's harder to dismiss an argument when a person was willing to sacrifice their own life to give voice to it.

replies.title: Re: Self-immolation
replies.content: Whether it functions as a death-curse or not depends on the intention of the person who does it. Whatever that person's intention, however, it's given enormous strength by such an act of self-sacrifice. The Buddhist monks who burnt themselves to end the Vietnam war, as far as I know, didn't intend a death-curse by it at all; they simply meant to direct every energy in their power to ending a brutal and useless war -- and of course they succeeded. It's not impossible that the total fiasco of the US occupation of Iraq turned out as badly as it did because of the similar acts of protesters -- in which case the US establishment may be in deep trouble.

replies.title: Re: Self-immolation
replies.content: Unfortunately the New York protester that lit himself on fire wasn't doing it with what the Buddhists monks had in mind. Reading the substack that he left behind, he was clearly going through a mental health episode triggered by world events, which he didn't really handle well.

replies.title: Re: Self-immolation
replies.content:
He was definitely off the wall, but we can't be sure what his focus was, and what impact that will have.
As for the earlier act of self-immolation, that intention was clear: freedom for Palestine, and defeat for Israel. Assuming JMG is right, such potent magic could have an effect on the outcome of the current clash in the Middle East; I've been thinking that there's a not insignificant chance that this will be Israel's last war, especially if it keeps escalating with Iran & BRICS. Buckle your seatbelt!

--xcalibur/djs

replies.title: Re: Self-immolation
replies.content: I also saw the story of the soldier and it had a different quality so to speak, though not less brutal. Interesting times...

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: What is it that gave you so much assurance in your own inner world rather than that of Society's? Do you think it was magic that gave you this inner dialogue or your inner dialog that drew you to magic and trust in yourself?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Partly, it's Aspergers syndrome. Much of the way that societies enforce conformity of belief and action on their inmates is through collective nonverbal communication -- that's the main force behind peer pressure, for example -- and since my brain doesn't have the mirror neurons that allow an ordinary person to pick up on that, most nonverbal communication goes zooming right by me. That kept me from absorbing a lot of the unspoken pressures that make most children set aside their own perceptions and believe what they're told to believe.

Then there was magic. Our society says it doesn't work; I learned very quickly, through my own experiences, that it does. There are various ways to resolve that kind of cognitive dissonance, but the way I resolved it -- the way occultists generally resolve it -- was to realize that our society is almost uniquely stupid about certain things, and magic is one of them.

Third is life experience. I'll be turning 62 in June; over the course of my life I've watched the officially accepted version of what's real spin around like a weathercock on a blustery day, and I've also seen quite a few things that were rejected as complete hogwash by the official spokescritters of government, business, and science turn out to be true. (It still baffles me that anyone can listen to an economist make a prediction and not simply start chuckling -- they're so reliably wrong!) Meanwhile, quite a few of the predictions I've made on the basis of simple common sense, in the face of heavy pressure from the defenders of the conventional wisdom, have turned out to be quite correct. That gives me a great deal of confidence that, while I'm still quite capable of being wrong, I'm not as reliably wrong as our society!

comments.title: past life updates
comments.content:
I've spoken before of my past life memories which bubbled up in my consciousness. Further study of the occult has brought more clarity to them, it seems. For example, regarding that female life in Medieval East Asia I mentioned before, I recently recalled that one of my favorite things back then was to skip around and sashay -- it feels really funny recalling that as an adult male, but there it is.

Anyway, even then I had the feeling that this wasn't my first human incarnation, but I couldn't grasp anything earlier, or so I thought. But now I have a major update, another memory I've accessed; although actually this has been in my head for awhile, it just took me awhile before I identified the recollection for what it really is.

So this time I was male, living in a much more forested Europe. I recall learning Latin, the prestige language of "refined civilization", but was always much more comfortable in my native tongue. Of the language I spoke, I remember just a bit -- a garbled fragment, perhaps -- but I remember, "gwith nur". With only that to go on, I started researching. It became clear to me that this was Celtic (given the gw consonant cluster). A rather difficult topic, given the conquest of Latin, but not impossible, since Celtic survived in fragmentary form around Britain & Ireland. I found my way to "A Pronouncing and Etymological Dictionary of the Gaelic Language" by Malcolm Maclennan. There, I found an entry for nur: "when, at the time". As for gwith, the closest equivalent was guth (which is fine, given the distance between my ancient Celtic and its descendant, modern Gaelic), and the entry for guth: "a voice, a word".

A spoken word out of time? No way that's a coincidence (an example in the dictionary is guth caointeach, a warning or hint from the dead; as spooky as that is, that might be reading too much into it). As it happens, I also thought about the Latin I studied then, and a word popped up: aquilam. Looking it up, it refers to the eagle standards carried by the Roman Legion! Once again, too good to be a coincidence.

So, this confirms that I've been in the human world for about 1800-2100 years, a massively revised estimate, and there's no certainty that even this was my first human life, although I sense that it was one of the earlier ones at least. Given this development, I have to ask again: how am I doing in my grand cycle? Did I mess up somewhere, or am I alright? Given that I've always been drawn to the supernatural & occult, am I getting close to graduating from the human race? You said that 800 years is about average (the time from my life as an asian girl living in a village til now), so how does 2000 years factor in?

Also, if you have any insights on the memories I've shared, and what they may be telling me, I'd be interested in that too.

--xcalibur/djs

replies.title: Re: past life updates
replies.content:
Latin survived in far from fragmentary form in Ireland straight through the Dark Ages -- it was from Ireland that Charlemagne imported learned monks to found schools in his empire in what is now France and western Germany, because they were more literate in Latin than anybody else in Europe at that time.

As for your broader question, why, I have no idea. Every soul traces its own trajectory through incarnation; the fact that you're still here after two millennia suggests that yes, you messed up good and proper somewhere in there, and the fact that you're now remembering past lives suggests that you're probably on the right track now, but your path is your own and nobody else can tell you what it means or where it leads.

replies.title: Re: past life updates
replies.content:

I'm aware of Latin surviving past Late Antiquity, and I've also read about Ireland being a potential nucleus of culture, which was overwhelmed by my Catholic Church and the Vikings. Could I be remembering Ireland of 1400-1500 years ago? Possibly, it's all rather vague. Even in that shorter time-frame, I'd assume I took a karmic wrong turn, but it's good to know I'm on the up & up.

I've mentioned on here before that I take meds for a rather deadly illness that came upon me seemingly out of nowhere. I wonder if that's karmic debt I'm paying off?

btw, minor correction -- I wasn't a girl living in a village, I was living in MY village. I still remember the connection & sense of belonging.

--xcalibur/djs

2) That's certainly possible.

"Hear first the four roots of all things: bright Zeus, life giving Hera, and Aidoneus, and Nestis who moistens the springs of men with her tears."

Aidoneus is an old name of Hades ("The Unseen One"), and Nestis is a name of Persephone (and maybe a cognate to Nepthys?).

comments.title: Benjamin Creme etc
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

For the first time in a very long time I now look forward to Mondays, thanks to your "Magic Mondays" blog! Am wondering your thoughts about the late Scottish esotericist/author and editor of Share International magazine, Benjamin Creme (d. 2016), a student of the Blavatasky/Bailey teachings who predicted the return (in physical body/mayavarupa) of the Maitreya-Christ or "World Teacher" in 1982 and again more recently claimed (according to his own unnamed master) that Christ has returned, though still anonymously/incognito, and is living in the Asian quarters of London patiently awaiting the opportune moment to "step forth publicly", followed similarly by other spiritual masters and the long-anticipated "externalization of the Hierarchy" (c.f. Bailey)? Who in your estimation is the alleged master with whom he was in contact for many decades, and would you consign Creme's teachings and visions/prophecies to the realm of "astral illusion/deception" even if well-meaning and sincere (which I believe he was)? Relatedly, do you subscribe to a hope or belief in any imminent (or longer-term) "public externalization" of advanced (beyond the human kingdom) Adepts/Masters on pla er earth? If not, in what other ways do you conceive of their roles in human lives, in planetary/cosmic evolution, in the evolution of consciousness, etc? Thank you!

Anthony
Pittsburgh, PA

replies.title: Re: Benjamin Creme etc
replies.content:
Benjamin Creme -- now there's a name I haven't heard in a good long while. I was familiar with him back when he and his followers were predicting 1982 as the date of the next World Teacher's arrival. I certainly grant that he was well-meaning and sincere, but he was also mistaken. The entire concept of the externalization of the hierarchy is what Eric Voegelin used to call an attempt to immanentize the eschaton -- that is, to mistake an eternal transcendent reality for something that will show up in matter, space and time.

It's a common error. Jesus of Nazareth spent his entire active career trying to get his apostles to understand that the Kingdom of God was within them, a transcendent reality that was always present, but they clearly kept on thinking that their teacher would kick out the Romans and establish a kingdom of this world -- and Christians are still waiting for a Second Coming that is always ready to happen within them if only they would listen to what the man said and follow his teachings.

As for the masters, they were once what we are now, and we will one day stand on their present level, when they have risen further still. The world as we experience it, with all its miseries and all its joys, is what it needs to be to further this stage of our spiritual evolution, and a public "externalization" would simply interfere with the lessons we need to learn. Thus I don't expect anything of the kind. Instead, the masters (or saints, or inner plane adepts, or whatever you want to call them) reach down constantly from their level, offering their help to all those who are willing to reach up in response. That's been true in every generation and will be true in every generation to come.

replies.title: Re: Benjamin Creme etc
replies.content: This makes a lot of sense. Rudolf Steiner used the metaphor of humans (their consciousness) "rising" to meet the Christ (descending into the etheric) "halfway". Of course, Yogananda has the book, which I providentially just chanced across on my local library's holdshelf today, "Second Coming: The Resurrection of the Christ Within You". I also recommend Tolstoy's "Gospel in Brief" and "The Kingdom of God is Within You", if you haven't read. And of course, most of all, Monty Python's movie "Life of Brian" for its important hagio-history lessons...

replies.title: Re: Benjamin Creme etc
replies.content:
Yes, exactly -- they'll meet us halfway, but we have to do the other half of the work ourselves.

Btw, I have to admit this is the first time I've seen the gospel according to Monty Python cited in a discussion of spiritual subjects!

comments.title: Golden Dawn Magick (GDM)
comments.content:
What is the next ritual in order that replaces the Middle Pillar in terms of power?

I have seen people get around this by doing a more complete version of the MP such as the full tree of life spherical invocation; invoking each sephirot in the order of the lightning flash and then invoking the archangel of that sphere.

Damien Echols himself has his own version in his book Angels and Archangels where he invokes the full hierarchy of angels from the 4 elements to the zodiacal Shem angels.

replies.title: Re: Golden Dawn Magick (GDM)
replies.content: The original Golden Dawn system didn't have one. It didn't have the Middle Pillar exercise as we now know it, for that matter -- it had a meditation assigned to the Portal grade, in which the practitioner visualizes the whole Tree of Life in his or her aura. Israel Regardie used that, some of Arthur Avalon's books, and the Chinese text The Secret of the Golden Flower to construct the Middle Pillar exercise, and it became standard in the tradition because it works so well. Several people, Damien among them, have introduced their own proposed workings to go the next step, and you can use one of those or experiment with something new yourself, as you wish.

replies.title: Re: Golden Dawn Magick (GDM)
replies.content:
I was hoping that such an exercise already existed which gave Echols his basis to derive from. My objection to his version is his incorporation of the Shem angels which I don't feel have anything to do with Kabbalah or the sephirots. They're Jewish angels (Bible) shoehorned into Christian Kabbalah.

Mark Stavish has a lightning flash exercise he posted in the 90s -- which invokes the entire tree -- and I think that can be the next step after the Middle Pillar.

replies.title: Re: Golden Dawn Magick (GDM)
replies.content: Yes, that's another option. Remember you can also modify any existing exercise and see how you like the results.

replies.title: Re: Golden Dawn Magick (GDM)
replies.content: I had no idea that Regardie had access to the Golden Flower, but it makes perfect sense given his interests.  So the Middle Pillar is a Cabalistically-flavored version of the microcosmic orbit after all?

replies.title: Re: Golden Dawn Magick (GDM)
replies.content:
1) An English translation of Richard Wilhelm's German version, with Carl Jung's impressively clueless commentary, was published in 1931. (Jung was very perceptive about psychology, but when it came to practical esotericism, he was like the once-famous guy who insisted that "The Hunting of the Snark" was about economic cycles.) The English edition made quite a splash in Western occult circles, not least because Jung was wildly popular in those circles already. Regardie published his first tentative version of the Middle Pillar exercise in The Art of True Healing in 1932, and then made the classic version the center of his 1938 book The Middle Pillar.

2) Er, not quite. It's closer to the macrocosmic circuit, anyway, since it goes all the way down to the feet!

comments.title: Movement Magic
comments.content:
Good Morning JMG,

Today, I am finishing a series of meditations using your Sacred Geometry Oracle. There is something I have noticed as a result of these meditations.

I can focus for an entire mediation period with zero distraction while figuring out how to do the constructions in my mind. Which is 20 minutes right now but I suspect I could likely go for an hour or more pretty easily. Interestingly, this level of focus is also very much like how I experience computer programming. Those sessions used to go for many hours on end.

1. The geometric figure construction process is very different from the experience of sensing for meaning during meditation. Is there value in this experience of building the figures in my mind or am I distracting myself?

Which leads me to my next question regarding what I will call movement magic for lack of a better term. Though it can have a mystical sense to it as well. Feeling a bit like I am connecting deeply with the foundations of the Cosmos.

There is this sense the movement of my hands and limbs are “weaving reality”. It has been showing up during my (highly esoteric) Aikido practices, in particular. Feels like weaving threads of energy.

2. Is this weaving of energy or reality a thing? Are there resources available to learn more about work others have done in this area?

Finally, a more general question. I feel like there are a lot of possibilities regarding development of the etheric body.

3. Is there a point where focusing on etheric body development becomes counter-productive? Or, maybe one’s time is better spent working on the other bodies?

My guess is if it is the case most of us have a long ways to go before we are at that point. Also, I am noticing working on one body can mix well with work with other bodies. Maybe this integration keeps one from overdoing it with a particular body?

I see my questions tend to lead me to more questions!

Thank you for your time and insights.

— Eric

3) The important thing is not to neglect the higher bodies. Since you practice daily meditation, you'll be fine -- that works with the astral body and mental sheath. It's a long, long road, and all our higher bodies can use vast amounts of work.

comments.content: Can you share what you know of Dion Fortune’s religious side? Was she an active member of an Anglican parish? Were there any particularly magic-friendly priests in the Church of England at that time? Did she ever get attacked by mainstream Christians? Thanks!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I don't happen to know the details of her religious activity, but I know she was an Anglican Christian and was familiar enough with the Book of Common Prayer to write a book of meditations on the Collects through the cycle of the year. Beyond that, I can't tell you much.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Dion Fortune's mother was a Christian Scientist, and Dion herself was raised in that religion as a child. She says this quite explicitly in one of her last articles in the Occult Review, which never made it in to any of her compiled volumes: "Christian Science is a thing I happen to be rather intimately acquainted with, having been brought up in it." (The article is titled "How Magic Works," and it appeared in the January 1935 issue.) Much of her article is how that religion teaches the practice of, as JMG has put it, "magic with the serial numbers filed off." And she makes it quite clear there that she no longer counts herself a Christian Scientist.

As JMG rightly points out, later in life she became quite familar with Church of England Christianity, and worked within its traditions. Christian Scientists are not baptized at any point in their lives, and so far as I know Dion never went so far as to become a baptized Christian.

There were priests in the Church of England who had strong esoteric interests, though I can't think of any specifics offhand. I expect most of those priests avoided using the loaded term "magic" for what they did.

comments.title: Most recent Uranus-Neptune conjunction
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Your mundane astrology posts on this month's outer planet conjunctions have been fascinating! I appreciate having some idea of the planetary flows of influence so that I can surf them more effectively. (I'm reading about the Law of Flows so that is affecting my choices of metaphor.)

With that in mind, I became curious about the most recent Uranus-Neptune conjunction. A basic mundane astrological expectation would be for disruptions (Uranus) in water flows (Neptune). Upon looking it up, for Washington DC it first occurred on Feb. 2, 1993, 7:01am, 19 Capricorn 34; again with both planets retrograde on Aug. 20, 1993, 8:23 am, 18 Capricorn 47; and finally with both planets direct on Oct. 24, 1993, 4:24am, 18 Capricorn 32. Since it takes Uranus 84 years to complete a cycle, it'll be about another 53 years before the next one.

What struck me so much about the 1993 conjunctions is the two big weather disruptions that happened that year, the Storm of the Century in March (https://www.wunderground.com/article/storms/winter/news/2023-03-09-superstorm-1993-anniversary-30-years) and the Great Flood of 1993 on the Mississippi and Missouri Rivers (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Great_Flood_of_1993). That's the sort of thing I would expect from the Uranus-Neptune conjunction. I would be quite interested in reading a delineation of this conjunction, if you have time and want to prepare a delineation for it.

replies.title: Re: Most recent Uranus-Neptune conjunction
replies.content: Fascinating. I'll certainly consider delineating it at some point.

replies.title: Re: Most recent Uranus-Neptune conjunction
replies.content: Minor quibble - it takes Uranus 84 years to complete an orbit around the Sun, but by the time he gets to the same point in that, Neptune has moved on.  The actual Uranus-Neptune conjunctions occur much more rarely (the next one isn't until 2165!).

comments.title: Two questions:
comments.content:
Hello and thank you again Mr. Greer!

Today I respectfully request your opinion on two things:

One-

One of the most consistent stories of spirit contact I get from people in the more "New-Age-y" side of the more spiritually minded segments of our culture is that a great many of these spirits are trying to encourage them to have more children so that they, specifically, can incarnate in human form. Usually this is from the various UFO-Channeling mediums, and often adjacent to the "Times of Dragons require Dragonslayers", "The great turning of the world is nigh!" arguments, and it's always left me in a head-scratching moment because aren't there too many bodies already? If we have a shortage of human souls, shouldn't the powers that be (I picture an overworked Cosmic Staffing Department) welcome them with overjoyed arms? And why would these spirits need humans to agree beforehand to become their parents, essentially?

I suppose it could be the Fae, up to their old tricks as always, especially as you commented that UFO phenomena are clearly at least partially some project of the Fair Folk... But why the prophesizing? Why would they want more humans? It's a question that's been nagging at me for a while.

Two-

Some of the more alarmist folks over in the astrology scene in various corners of the internet have seized upon both the Saturn-Neptune Conjunction *and* the prophecies of Rudolf Steiner to suggest that the conjunction in question could herald the Incarnation of Ahriman. Particularly, this has been brought to my attention because a friend recommended this as I had mentioned your point that the last time a Saturn-Neptune conjunction happened in the early degrees of Aries was sometime around the 6th century BC, when Zoroastrianism was founded, and Ahriman is the principal antagonist of Zoroastrian mythology. That this would be right around the time the "Brain Chip" technology is supposed to hit the mainstream(again, speculation), has people reaching a state of... Well, I don't want to call it "Hysteria", but pretty close.

I try to convince people that Steiner's prophecies are best taken metaphorically and all, but as you're the most level-headed expert on Steiner, Occultism, and Mundane Astrology I know of, anything that you could say to at least inform this narrative would certainly be appreciated. How much weight do you give all of this? In your opinion, what, if anything, would the Saturn Neptune conjunction mean in the context of Steiner's views?

replies.title: Re: Two questions:
replies.content:
1) Oh, it's quite possible that certain spirits are trying to exploit the surplus of human bodies at the moment to get into human incarnation, by talking prospective parents into making a bargain with them. That's not something that benevolent spirits would do, but not all spirits are benevolent -- and very few people in the New Age scene have any notion of how to tell the difference.

2) Steiner's visions are a grab bag of genuine insights and astral static; his belief system didn't allow him to recognize that visions can be metaphorical or simply garbled. I don't recommend trying to talk believers out of their pet apocalypse, though. People love to think that some outside force is going to pop them out of the miserable lives so many of them have made for themselves; they get the same kind of rush out of apocalyptic fantasies that other people get from horror movies, and it's quite addictive. Once this conjunction goes by and nothing happens, they'll find something else -- just as the people who were yelling about 2012 suddenly started talking about other things when December 21 of that year passed without incident.

comments.title: animal karma
comments.content:
JMG,

I realize that this question calls for speculation on your part--on a basically unknowable subject, but here goes.

Thinking about recent news story re. a human recipient of a transplanted kidney from specially bred pigs, I wondered what effect this would have on the karma of the pig? On one hand, the pig didn't choose to make the sacrifice, OTH it has materially aided another being. Such a specific form of sacrifice was not possible before modern technology, but I suspect that there may be occult lore about animals who sacrifice themselves to save a human, such as by fighting a predator. Or there may be some beliefs about whether animals are rewarded in some way for being sacrificed in ritual. I have read some philosophers on the desirability of sacrifice as a practice from the POV of benefits to the humans, but recall nothing on this aspect.

Thanks for your time and efforts on this blog.


Rita

replies.title: Re: animal karma

Of course, it is possible that it was addressed by Greek or Roman philosophers but that those texts were not preserved by the Church since it was irrelevant once Pagan religion was suppressed. OTh the lack of any such teachings may reflect a general tendency of Western philosophy to draw a firmer line between humanity and the other animals than is the case in other systems, including Hinduism and Buddhism.

Thanks again for your input.

Rita

comments.title: Geomancy

What to do when Cauda Draconis appears in the first house? Your book says to redo the chart after a couple of hours, but isn't redoing a chart on the sane question discouraged in every divination system or do I have it wrong?

replies.title: Re: Geomancy
replies.content: I've come to see this, like the other "oh no" figure in the 1st house, Rubeus, as communicating something important. Cauda Draconis says, "too late, it's all over, don't waste your time with this question." Rubeus says, "the querent is lying to you;" if you cast the reading for yourself, it says, "You're not being honest with yourself." That's why it's traditional not to continue the reading when these figures appear in the 1st.

comments.title: Remembering past lives
comments.content: How does one go about recalling their past lives?  Do you start to see visions in meditation, see them in scrying, dreams, or what have you? I am getting a vague sense about my last incarnation the more I meditate but I am interested in specific details.

replies.title: Re: Remembering past lives
replies.content: Don't try to force the process of recalling past lives. It's something that comes naturally with the ripening of spiritual practice; if you force it, you're likely to mess up the process with random astral imagery. Just keep meditating and pay attention to that vague sense, and let things unfold in their own time.
